fuzzy_prd: We need a single cross-platform command-line tool that wraps a set of common shell utilities — copying files, removing files, listing directory contents, printing text, searching for patterns, changing permissions, testing whether a file exists, and a few others — behind one consistent front end. Right now our build scripts break on Windows because the underlying utilities differ across platforms. The tool should take a command name as the first argument, run the corresponding utility, and report exit status, standard output, and standard error in a uniform format so it can be embedded in any build pipeline. When something goes wrong at the routing level (no command supplied, an unrecognized command, or a command that the tool explicitly refuses to support — handle those the same way the blacklist module does) it should fail with a dedicated status code distinct from the underlying utility's own failure codes. There should be a global silence flag that suppresses error output without hiding the exit status. We also need the tool to support piping input into commands that accept it from a stream, and a stream-substitution command that accepts the familiar s/pattern/replacement/ expression syntax including escaped slashes, global replacement, and an option to rewrite the source file in place. The tool should also load optional extension modules listed in a configuration file in the working directory, and raise distinct errors if that configuration file cannot be parsed or if a listed module cannot be found.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: main(stdin: JSON object with args/files/dirs/stdin/config/action) -> stdout/stderr/exit/file/mode report lines for shx command scenarios

trigger_keywords: what is the dedicated error exit code for routing failures
what exit status does the tool use for usage errors
what is the SHX_ERROR exit code value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dedicated usage-error exit code is 27. This applies to missing_command, unknown_command, unsupported_command, empty_pattern, plugin_not_found, and config_unreadable errors. It is distinct from underlying command failures (e.g., ls on a missing file returns exit code 2).

trigger_keywords: what error format is used for routing errors
what output format for unknown command errors
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Usage/routing errors produce no stdout and no stderr fields — only an 'error=<category>' line and, where applicable, 'command=<json-encoded name>' or 'plugin=<json-encoded name>'. The command/plugin name is JSON-encoded (quoted string). Exit code is 27.

trigger_keywords: what error code is used when the sed pattern is empty
what happens when the substitution pattern is empty string
how does an empty regex in sed fail
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An empty search pattern in a sed expression (e.g., s//foo/g) raises error=empty_pattern with no stdout/stderr fields and an implicit non-zero exit.

trigger_keywords: when does the tool consume piped stdin
how does the stdin reading decision work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Piped stdin is consumed only when ALL of: (1) the input is not an interactive terminal (tty=false), (2) the command is one that accepts stdin, and (3) fewer file operands were supplied than the command requires. If any condition fails, stdin is not consumed. For example, 'cat' with no file and tty=false consumes stdin; 'cat file.txt' with tty=false does not.

trigger_keywords: what config file name does the tool look for
where is the plugin configuration file
what is the name of the working-directory configuration file
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The tool looks for a configuration file named '.shxrc.js' (or the CONFIG_FILE constant value) in the current working directory. If present, it loads plugins listed under the 'plugins' key of that file.

trigger_keywords: what are the explicitly unsupported commands
what commands are on the blacklist
what commands does the tool refuse to run
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The CMD_BLACKLIST includes 'cd' and other commands that would mutate the runner's own process state. Requesting any blacklisted command returns error=unsupported_command with the command name and exit code 27.

Full portable shx command-wrapper surface: echo, cp, rm, mkdir, ls/show_files, pwd/cwd placeholder, cat/stdin, grep, sed substitution and in-place edits, chmod octal/symbolic/show_modes, test conditions, true/false, --silent behavior, usage errors, plugin config parsing, missing plugin errors, and tty/stdin decision behavior. Public test cases are authoritative for exact stdout/stderr JSON encoding, exit codes, trailing newlines, file maps, and mode strings.

The adapter reads one JSON object. Command scenarios use args plus files/dirs/stdin/show_files/show_modes as needed; plugin/stdin scenarios may use config/action/tty. Preserve exact stdout, stderr, exit, files, dirs, modes, and cwd-placeholder output from public_test_cases.

The embedded public_test_cases are part of oracle_data and are authoritative for exact input shapes, action/operation names, stdout line order, whitespace, error categories, and deterministic formatting. When prose and expected_output differ, expected_output is the pass-critical contract.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle those the same way the blacklist module does
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The CMD_BLACKLIST array defined in src/config.js lists explicitly unsupported command names; any command in this list is rejected with error=unsupported_command rather than being routed to the shell utility

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that detail. Please refer to the start.md specification and the public test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for authoritative requirements.

feature1_echo.json.description: Run the line-printing command, which writes its space-joined operands followed by a single trailing newline to standard output and exits successfully. With no operands it still emits one newline.

feature2_side_effect_no_output.json.description: Commands that perform a filesystem side effect but yield no textual result write nothing to standard output or standard error and exit successfully.

feature3_missing_command.json.description: Invoking the runner with no command name reports a missing-command usage error and exits with the dedicated usage-error status.

feature4_unknown_command.json.description: Requesting a command name that is not recognized reports an unknown-command error naming the offending token and exits with the usage-error status.

feature5_unsupported_command.json.description: Some command names are explicitly unsupported by the runner; requesting one reports an unsupported-command error naming it and exits with the usage-error status.

feature6_command_failure.json.description: When the underlying command fails the runner forwards the command's own non-zero exit status together with its diagnostic message on standard error.

cases.expected_output:
exit_code=2
stdout=""
stderr="ls: no such file or directory: fakeFileName\n"

feature7_silent_mode.json.description: A global silent flag suppresses diagnostic output: a failing command still returns its non-zero exit status, but its error message is withheld from standard error.

cases.expected_output:
exit_code=2
stdout=""
stderr="ls: no such file or directory: fakeFileName\n"

feature8_boolean_commands.json.description: Commands whose result is a boolean condition print nothing; the outcome is conveyed solely through the exit status.

feature9_pwd_newline.json.description: The working-directory command prints the absolute path of the current directory followed by a trailing newline.

feature10_stdin_pipe.json.description: When input is supplied on the pipe, line-filtering commands consume it as their input.

feature11_true_false.json.description: Two always-available commands report a fixed condition with no output.

feature12_1_sed_basic.json.description: Stream substitution accepts s/<pattern>/<replacement>/ and replaces only the first match per line by default.

feature12_2_sed_global_inplace.json.description: With the global suffix every match is replaced; with the in-place flag the result is written back to the source file.

feature12_3_sed_escaped_slashes.json.description: Forward slashes inside the pattern or replacement may be escaped with a backslash.

feature12_4_sed_backslash_to_slash.json.description: An escaped backslash in the pattern matches a literal backslash.

feature12_5_sed_empty_replacement.json.description: An empty replacement deletes matched text.

feature12_6_sed_empty_pattern.json.description: An empty search pattern is rejected.

feature12_7_sed_filename_like_expression.description: Only the first operand that looks like a substitution expression is treated as one.

feature13_grep_regex.json.description: Pattern search over a file prints every line that matches the given regular expression.

files.file.txt:
1st line
foo
f
does not match
something foo

feature14_chmod_modes.json.description: Changing file permissions accepts both absolute octal modes and symbolic mode expressions.

feature15_plugin_not_found.json.description: If a listed plugin cannot be found, the runner raises a plugin-load error identifying the missing plugin.

feature16_config_unreadable.json.description: If the configuration file cannot be parsed, the runner raises a configuration error.

feature17_stdin_read_decision.json.description: The tool decides whether to consume piped input based on tty status, command type, and file operand count.
